proof_sketch: The mutation changes `global_col < N` to `global_col <= N`, allowing a load from B[global_row * N + N] when global_col == N. For N=5921, TILE_N=128, the last block column (block_col=46) has columns 5888..6015; global_col==N occurs at col=33. This out-of-bounds read is reachable, but the loaded value is from memory beyond the tensor's storage. The forward() calls contiguous().cuda() on inputs, creating fresh allocations; thus the memory after B is not controlled by input values. All 112 random seeds and 45 deterministic stress tests produced bitwise identical results, implying that the out-of-bounds memory reads as zero (or matches the zero-padding) in all scenarios. No value pattern can force a non-zero value at that location because it is not part of the input tensors.

No value pattern can force a non-zero value at that location because it is not part of the input tensors.",
  "survival_reason": "The mutant survived because the out-of-bounds read introduced by the `<=` operator accesses memory that is not part of any input tensor and whose value is not determined by the contract's variable-value specification. Under the fixed-shape contract, we can only vary the values within the tensor shapes, not the memory layout or contents of adjacent allocations. Since the kernel always receives contiguous copies via `.contiguous().cuda()`, we cannot control what lies immediately after B. All tested value distributions resulted in that memory being zero (or indistinguishable from zero), making the mutant's output bitwise identical to the original. The kill strategy suggested in Layer 3 (placing B in a larger buffer with distinctive tail) fails because contiguous() would copy only the slice, discarding the extra data. Therefore, no value-level test can force a difference.",
